title: Apothecary-Style Kitchen Cabinets
summary: A build-to-fit plywood base cabinet with a stacked apothecary-style face frame of narrow drawer and door boards, sized to your own kitchen run.
description:
This is a technique, more than a fixed set of dimensions — the plan is written so you measure your own appliances and cabinet run and adjust the carcass width accordingly, rather than building to a single number. That makes it a genuinely useful reference for a custom kitchen, but it also means you do more of the planning work up front than on a fixed-size cabinet: decide your cabinet's overall width before cutting anything, and don't span more than 3 feet per cabinet (less if you're topping it with a heavy stone or concrete counter) or the carcass will flex under load.

The apothecary look comes entirely from the face frame and door layout — a grid of narrow 1x2-framed door and drawer boards stacked and gapped an even 1/8" apart, rather than one or two large doors. Build and fit every door and drawer front before you attach the face frame to the carcass; it's much easier to true up a stack of small doors on the bench than to fight their alignment once the frame is permanently on. Drawers wider than about 24" start to bind on their slides, so keep that as a hard ceiling when you're deciding how to divide up your own cabinet width.
timeLabel: 8–11 hrs, more if building multiple cabinets in a run

tools.note: For ripping plywood to your own cabinet's custom width.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", finished, sheet
materials.note: Ripped to your cabinet's own width — 23-1/4" strips size a 24"-overall cabinet, but measure and adjust to your run.

materials.name: Cabinet door hinges
materials.note: One per door/drawer grid opening — adjust count to your own layout.

steps.title: Decide your cabinet's width and build the carcass
steps.body: Measure your appliances and cabinet run first, and set your cabinet's overall width before cutting anything — this plan is written to be adjusted, not built to one fixed number. The carcass is 3/4" plywood with scrap 1x material (or ripped plywood strips) as top supports. Use 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket screws throughout; place the pocket holes on the outside faces if you know that side will be hidden against a neighboring cabinet. Don't span more than 3 feet per cabinet section — less if you're planning a heavy stone or concrete countertop — and keep any drawer sections under 24" wide so the slides don't bind.

steps.title: Build the toekick base
steps.body: Cut and screw or nail the toekick base together. Place boards under every carcass divider so the countertop's weight transfers straight down to the floor rather than through unsupported plywood. Pocket holes, wood screws, or framing nails all work for this joint. On installation, the easiest approach is 2" screws through the bottom of the cabinet into the base; alternatively, attach from underneath with pocket holes set for 3/4" stock and 1-1/4" screws, even though you're driving into 2x4 stock.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: The face frame covers every exposed plywood edge on the front of the cabinet. Confirm your 1x2s are a true 1-1/2" wide before building — actual widths vary by supplier. Use 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws, and glue to assemble the frame, then attach it to the front of the plywood carcass with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. Build and fit your doors and drawers before attaching the face frame — it's much easier to true up the door grid on the bench, and you can even pre-hang the door hinges to the face frame before it goes on the carcass.

steps.title: Build the door and drawer grid
steps.body: Arrange all of your door and drawer boards face-down inside the face frame opening, and use 1x2 boards to connect them into a single grid. Keep an even 1/8" gap around every door and drawer face, and between the boards themselves — that consistent reveal is what gives this style its apothecary look. Assemble with 1-1/4" screws and glue.

steps.title: Add the back and hang doors
steps.body: Attach the 1/4" plywood back panel with glue and nails or staples, then hang each door in the grid with a hinge.

images.alt: Gray apothecary-style kitchen cabinet with a grid of small narrow doors and drawers
